data.title: Okta: Unmissable Value In Today's Market
data.description:
With the markets shifting their attention to the potential for Fed rate cuts and the narrative moving away from macroeconomic and geopolitical pressures, the S&P 500 is nearing YTD highs again, and for many stocks, particularly in the large-cap space, valuations are stretched to uncomfortable highs. To me, these conditions make it even more imperative for investors to rotate more of our portfolios into "growth at a reasonable price" stocks.

Okta (NASDAQ:OKTA), in my view, is one of the best plays here. Okta is a terrific example of how quickly a former market darling can transition into a penalty-box stock. Though Okta is up more than 20% since the start of January, the stock has only barely shaken off losses in 2024, owing to sales execution missteps that plagued the company's growth rates and net retention rates. With evidence of much stronger sales execution this year, however, Okta is well poised to rebound.

I last wrote a buy opinion on Okta in March, when the company was still trading around ~$100 per share. Since then, Okta has slid somewhat on its recent failure to boost its full-year outlook. At the same time, however, I appreciate the fact that Okta is making sharp progress at fixing its go-to-market flukes while at the same time driving major improvements in its operating margins. With very undemanding valuation multiples relative to its TAM and to its growth rates versus peers, I am reiterating my buy opinion here.

As a reminder for investors who are newer to Okta, here is what I view as the core long-term bull thesis for this stock:

  • Best in class identity management platform. Okta is broadly considered to be the leader in the single sign-on category, helping companies to securely manage employee profiles and logins across both internal applications and cloud-based applications.
  • Huge $80 billion TAM. Okta estimates its global TAM at $80 billion, indicating that the company's current ~$3 billion in annualized revenue is less than 4% penetrated into its global opportunity. Though all attention has shifted to AI, we also can't forget the tailwinds of more and more companies migrating apps away from on-prem and into the cloud, which is requiring coordination in single sign-on from the likes of Okta.
  • Excellent RPO growth. Okta's remaining performance obligations, which is a primary measure of backlog for many enterprise software companies, is growing more quickly than its revenue - which signals that the company may be able to achieve revenue acceleration in the near term.
  • Growth/profitability balance. Okta manages to achieve low double digits revenue growth stacked on top of high 20s pro forma operating margins, which positions the company extremely close to the Rule of 40 (a commonly used measure of growth and profitability balance for software companies that adds revenue growth to operating margin).

Despite Okta's strengths, the company's penalty box status since last year (owing to choppy sales execution) has cast the pall of discounted valuation multiples on this stock, which still persist today. At current share prices just under $100, Okta trades at a market cap of $17.18 billion. After we net off the $2.73 billion of cash and $858 million of convertible debt on Okta's latest balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $15.31 billion.

For FY26 (the year for Okta ending in January 2026), the company has maintained its guidance at $2.85-$2.86 billion in revenue, or 9-10% y/y growth. Its 27% FCF margin also implies ~$772 million in FCF

This positions Okta's valuation at:

  • 5.4x EV/FY26 revenue
  • 19.8x EV/FY25 revenue

When we stack up Okta against other software companies that are expected to grow revenue in the low double digits, we find that there is at least a one-turn discount to peers like Workday:

Stay long here and hold out for a broader rebound in Okta.

Q1 download

Let's now go through Okta's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

Okta's revenue grew 12% y/y to $688 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $680 million (+10% y/y) while decelerating only one point from 13% y/y growth in Q4.

Off the bat, we do note it was a rather mixed quarter for sales execution. One of the metrics that Okta continues to disappoint on is net retention rates, which weakened one point further to 106%. This being said, Okta isn't alone in the software industry in facing waning net retention rates: as companies slow headcount growth and some even go to the extreme of executing sharp layoffs, companies like Okta that price their products on a per-seat basis have been under more pressure to keep up the same pace of expansion.

That being said, the company notes that it is building up a healthy pipeline, and the recent segmentation of the company's sales teams on a product basis (between Okta and its subsidiary Auth0 which it acquired in 2021) has yielded positive results. Per CFO Brett Tighe's remarks on the Q1 earnings call:

We entered the first quarter with the previously announced realignment of our go-to-market team, which further specialized our sales force into Okta sellers and Auth0 sellers. While it's still too early to judge the overall success of this realignment, we're encouraged by some of the early signals in the Q1 results. In particular, Auth0 performed quite well following a record Q4. We were also pleased to see pipeline strengthening throughout March and April.

We remain confident that increased go-to-market specialization will yield long-term benefits for Okta and our customers. Adding to our confidence is the recent performance we're seeing in the parts of our business that had already been specialized. At the beginning of last year, we moved the team focused on the U.S. SMB market to a hunter-farmer model. That team performed well in Q1 and underscores how specialization can drive improvements over time.

Another area that has been specialized for some time is the U.S. public sector vertical, which has been an area of strength over the last few years. The strength has been a direct result of Okta's strategic commitment and investments in the U.S. public sector. Our public sector team had a strong Q1 as two of our top three and four of our top 10 deals were in the public sector, including the federal deal we called out in our posted commentary."

Evidence of Okta's improving deal momentum is evident in the company's remaining performance obligations (essentially a measure of backlog). Total RPO grew 21% y/y to $4.08 billion (indicating a slew of multi-year deals), while current RPO (which is the portion that will convert to revenue within one year) grew 14% y/y; decelerating only one point from 15% y/y RPO growth in Q4. Both of these metrics are well above revenue growth, and a healthy signal that Okta's growth has a good chance of holding at its current low double digit pace, if not even accelerate (recall that Okta's full-year guidance has growth dipping to the 9-10% range for the full year, which I find conservative with room for upside revision).

The company has also maintained excellent cost discipline. As shown in the chart below, Okta's pro forma operating margins rose 5 points y/y to 27%, while nominal pro forma operating income dollars grew 38% y/y to $184 million.

Risks and key takeaways

Of course, there are also risks that investors need to keep in mind with Okta. In particular, we should monitor closely how AI impacts use cases for Okta. As companies like Salesforce begin to introduce agentic AI products, login by human employees may eventually give way to more tasks being performed directly by agentic AI - and in this space, companies like SailPoint (SAIL) have built up more of a brand around securely provisioning data access to AI personas. We should also be wary of the macro environment: despite recent optimism in the stock market, big companies are slashing headcount, which is a direct hit to Okta's net revenue retention rates.

That being said, I think these risks are already quite well captured into the stock's below-market ~5x forward revenue multiple. Take advantage of the opportunity to buy Okta while it's still cheap.

data.title: VanEck Mid-June Bitcoin ChainCheck
data.description:
Bitcoin sets new highs and holds strong above $100K as Ordinals fade and onchain activity slows. Equity markets reward treasury-heavy BTC proxies over operational miners.

Please note that VanEck has exposure to bitcoin.

Three key takeaways for mid-May to mid-June:

  1. Price Resilience in a Volatile Macro Backdrop: Bitcoin (BTC) set new all-time highs above $110K, though geopolitical tension briefly pushed BTC as low as $98K on June 22nd, marking a six-week low.
  2. Onchain Activity Slows: Transaction volume and fee revenue declined, with Ordinals volumes hitting year-to-date lows, highlighting Bitcoin’s growing reliance on off-chain adoption.
  3. Treasury Plays Outperform… for Now: Metaplanet’s (OTC:MTPLF) +125% rally highlights recent investor preference for balance sheet BTC exposure over operational mining models, though leadership could shift with some valuations looking stretched.

Bitcoin’s Price Action

Bitcoin (BTC-USD) Borrowing Rates are Near YTD Lows Despite All-Time High Prices

Bitcoin quietly carved new all-time highs this month, reaching nearly $112K on May 22 and trading above $110K again on June 9. Yet, in the wake of escalating U.S.-Iran tensions over the weekend, BTC briefly fell to ~$98K, its lowest level in over a month, before stabilizing back above $100K.

Despite this volatility, 30-day moving averages remain elevated at ~$107K. Interestingly, borrowing rates have cooled to 7%, about 50% lower than at the start of 2025, indicating neutral market sentiment and increased stability.

While future conditions remain uncertain, Bitcoin’s evolving role as both a high-beta tech proxy and a macro hedge may allow it to benefit across multiple scenarios. In peace, improved risk appetite could favor high-growth assets like BTC. In conflict, rising fiscal deficits and currency concerns may bolster the asset’s appeal among reserve-seeking investors.

Bitcoin’s Market Cap Dominance Hit Multi-Year Highs in June

Bitcoin Dominance and Institutional Flows

Bitcoin’s market dominance rose another 1% in June, setting a new 30-day moving average high for this cycle. This continued rise reflects Bitcoin’s strengthening role as the preferred crypto asset among institutional investors, even as altcoins remain largely trapped in speculative, retail-driven narratives.

Since the 2021–2022 altcoin cycle, Bitcoin has reclaimed nearly all market share gains made by other crypto assets. The momentum in dominance supports the view that Bitcoin has matured into a digital store-of-value -- functionally “digital gold”-- for both public and private institutions.

Unlike prior cycles, investors today can access leveraged BTC exposure through public equities. Strategy (MSTR), for example, offers deep liquidity and capital efficiency, while smaller entrants like Semler Scientific (SMLR) provide high-volatility trading opportunities. These structures appear to absorb speculative demand that might have previously flowed into altcoins.

While ETH and SOL treasury strategies show early promise, Bitcoin continues to command the lion’s share of institutional flows, supported by its dominant liquidity, simple narrative, and macro positioning. Altcoins, by contrast, have struggled to attract sustained inflows, with market activity largely rotational and retail-driven. As a result, Bitcoin appears to be pulling in net new capital, while altcoins operate in a more insular environment, reinforcing BTC’s status as the foundation of institutional crypto exposure.

Onchain Performance: Activity Slows as Offchain Adoption Grows

Daily Bitcoin Transactions and Fees Hovered Near YTD Lows in June

While market-facing indicators such as price action, futures, flows, and institutional positioning remain supportive, Bitcoin’s on-chain metrics point to softening activity. Over the past 30 days:

  • The Bitcoin network processed an average of 364,000 transactions per day, down 8% month-over-month and 42% year-over-year.
  • Daily fee revenue averaged $641K, down 57% year-to-date.
  • In contrast, block rewards earned by miners remain elevated at approximately $45M per day, assuming 144 blocks/day at $100K per BTC.
  • This means that, on an annualized basis, transaction fees account for just 1.4% of the estimated $16.4B in yearly block rewards, a stark mismatch between network usage and price performance.

This disconnect may persist, especially as Bitcoin adoption increasingly occurs offchain through financial products like ETPs and corporate treasuries, rather than through native blockchain activity. Looking ahead, the scheduled block reward halvings in 2028 and 2032 will further reduce miner revenue, raising structural questions about the long-term sustainability of security incentives if fee generation doesn’t materially recover.

Ordinals ("Bitcoin NFTs") Activity Fell To 2025 Lows in June

Ordinals Cool Off

A major factor behind Bitcoin’s weaker on-chain metrics is the collapse in activity related to Ordinals, which allowed users to inscribe digital content onto individual Satoshis—effectively enabling Bitcoin-native NFTs.

After launching in early 2023, Ordinals briefly revitalized Bitcoin’s base layer, bringing in unprecedented retail demand. Transaction volumes surged, fees spiked, and Bitcoin—which historically lacked the smart contract capabilities of chains like Ethereum (ETH-USD) and Solana (SOL-USD)—saw renewed relevance as an application layer.

From late 2023 through early 2024, Ordinals helped narrow the perceived gap between Bitcoin and more expressive chains by enabling digital collectibles, meme assets, and experimental DeFi primitives on BTC. But in June, activity collapsed: transaction volumes fell 39% month-over-month, marking new 2025 lows and the weakest performance since the latest wave of interest began in late 2024.

The decline reflects both cooling speculative interest and structural challenges in sustaining high-fee on-chain activity. While the protocol was an important moment for Bitcoin’s cultural evolution, its volatility underscores the difficulty of building persistent user demand on a chain not optimized for programmability.

Bitcoin Pure-Play Stocks

Metaplanet (+125%)

Metaplanet stood out as a top performer among Bitcoin pure-plays this month, up 125% MoM. The company announced three purchases of Bitcoin: On May 19th, June 2nd, and June 16th, Metaplanet acquired 1,004, 1,088, and 1,112 Bitcoin, respectively, bringing its total holdings to 10,000 BTC. The company also announced its 2025-2027 Bitcoin Plan, targeting 210,000 BTC by 2027. While we flag that Metaplanet’s NAV Premium to basic shares (~7.1x) remains elevated compared to Strategy’s (~1.8x) and Semler Scientific’s (~1.1x), we recognize the company’s dominance as a Bitcoin treasury company in the Japanese and broader Asian markets, in addition to certain tax and regulatory advantages it offers over spot Bitcoin.

Strategy (-11%)

In late May, Strategy announced its latest Bitcoin acquisition, adding 4,020 to bring its total to 580,250 BTC. On June 6th, Strategy priced a $980M public offering of 10.00% Series A Perpetual Preferred Stock ('STRD'), with proceeds earmarked for further Bitcoin acquisitions and general corporate purposes. The non-cumulative preferred shares, structured to pay quarterly cash dividends when declared, represent Strategy’s latest innovation in capital structure to scale BTC exposure without issuing common equity. While MSTR’s 1M performance was negative, we note that its YTD (+28%) performance still largely outpaces Bitcoin’s (+7%), and that its NAV premium suggests more downside protection than Metaplanet’s.

CleanSpark (-7%)

CleanSpark (CLSK) doubled its BTC treasury year-over-year, now holding 12,502 BTC. In May, it produced 694 BTC, selling only 293.5. While CleanSpark holds more BTC than Metaplanet, its market cap is only one-third as large, suggesting either a steep discount on miners or a premium on treasuries.

Notably, CleanSpark has avoided equity dilution since November 2024, a rare feat in the mining space, and continues to scale through localized energy strategies. Still, the market’s broader view on mining economics remains skeptical.

Marathon Digital (-12%)

Despite having the highest energized hash rate (58.3 EH/s) and the second-largest BTC treasury (49,179 BTC), Marathon fell 12% MoM, underperforming other pure-plays. While the company has begun building out its own infrastructure, about 45% of its operational hashrate still comes from hosted arrangements, which can limit margins and strategic flexibility, especially when compared to fully integrated miners. Marathon’s past reliance on third-party hosting and its history of equity dilution continue to weigh on investor sentiment. That overhang could persist unless the company transitions more fully to a self-operated model or demonstrates progress in diversifying its revenue base beyond core mining.

data.title: AMG TimesSquare Mid Cap Growth Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
The AMG TimesSquare Mid Cap Growth Fund (MUTF:TQMIX) (Class I) returned -3.48% for the first quarter of 2025, compared with -7.12% for the benchmark, the Russell Midcap ® Growth Index. For the 12 months ending March 31, 2025, the Fund returned −2.74%, while the benchmark returned 3.57%.

Quarterly Update

Expectations for businesses and markets were broadly positive as 2025 began. Hopes for a pro-business, lighter regulatory environment lifted global markets in January. However, delays and uncertainties regarding U.S. policies tempered markets in February, and the U.S. pulled back sharply in March (to say nothing about the early part of April).

  • In the first quarter, broad-based weakness of U.S. equities (-5% return for the Russell 3000® Index) lagged overseas markets ( 7% for the MSCI EAFE Index and 3% for the MSCI Emerging Markets Index).

Even before the market's tariff tantrum at the start of April, there were inflationary pressures. Global Purchasing Manager Indices (PMIs) showed moderating New Order rates and higher Prices Paid/Output for manufacturing and service activities.

  • While value indices outperformed growth, both Growth and Value had positive returns among global risk factors. It was the negative showing from Beta that overwhelmed everything else.

Fund Update

Amidst this negative market environment, the Fund outperformed the Russell Midcap ® Growth Index in the first quarter.

In the communication services sector, we generally prefer entertainment or media-related companies that either benefit from providing advertisers with a different target audience or offer related services. Gaining 7% was the image-based social media network for discovery and planning, Pinterest, Inc. (PINS) Pinterest exceeded expectations for revenues and earnings thanks to better user engagement and improved advertising placements, which led to higher monetization levels. The numbers of monthly active users also increased by over 10% (to 553 mm).

Our preferences in the consumer-oriented sectors lean toward value-oriented or specialty retailers, franchise models, premium brands, or support services for other consumer companies. One benefit was the specialty retailer and supplier of auto parts, tools, supplies, and accessories, O'Reilly Automotive (ORLY). A steady increase in same-store sales led O'Reilly's revenues above expectations. The company's management tempered its 2025 guidance, which seemed well-telegraphed given broader consumer caution, and its shares climbed by 21%.

While recent results were in-line with expectations, Ross Stores (ROST) provided forward guidance that was less than anticipated. The operator of discount apparel and home fashion outlets anticipates a slow start to the year attributable in part to the new CEO's understandable caution amidst present economic conditions. That marked down Ross's shares by −15%.

Warehouse club operator BJ's Wholesale (BJ) bested expectations for revenues and growth with better same-store sales growth. BJ's forward guidance was in-line with projections and the company plans an accelerated pace of new store openings over the next two years. Its shares climbed by 28% this quarter.

In the financials sector we tend to avoid banks that face credit deterioration or rising deposit costs, preferring either asset managers, specialized insurance companies, or financial technology providers. On the plus side there was an 8% gain from Allstate Corporation (ALL), which provides personal property and casualty insurance, life insurance, as well as retirement and investment products. Revenues and earnings were higher than anticipated, propelled by underlying loss ratio beats across Homeowners and auto insurance as well as lower than expected catastrophe losses. Notably, the company highlighted that it expects policies-in-force to grow in 2025.

Offsetting that was a - 24% showing from private equity manager TPG Inc. (TPG) While distributable earnings were above expectations, the underlying fee-related revenues were below. Some of that disconnect came from the consolidation of recently acquired Angelo Gordon in TPG's numbers, which will be measured on an apples-to-apples basis going forward.

Elsewhere, we benefited from a 12% gain from the online brokerage platform Robinhood Markets, Inc. (HOOD) At the start of the year, increased monthly trading volumes for options, significant gains in equity trading, and both were eclipsed by the growth of customers trading cryptocurrencies. Mid-quarter, Robinhood reported surging revenue and earnings by exceeding $1B of quarterly revenues for the first time, and its premium Robinhood Gold membership ranks swelled.

Our preferences among health care stocks are those companies providing novel therapies for unmet needs that deserve premium pricing, or specialized service providers. We were rewarded with a 52% return from Intra-Cellular Therapies. A developer of small molecule treatments for neurological disorders, we added Intra-Cellular to the Fund in December. In January, the company agreed to an acquisition offer from Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), and we sold our shares in February.

Cencora, Inc. (COR), which distributes pharmaceutical and medical products to pharmacies, hospitals, and other health care providers, saw its price improve by 24%. Cencora's revenues and earnings each exceeded expectations, and management further increased forward guidance to reflect its business momentum. Later in the quarter, Cencora again lifted its outlook though that did not include future benefits from a recent acquisition.

Many of our industrials positions provide necessary business-to-business operational services, highly technical components, equipment enabling automation and efficiency improvements, or essential infrastructure services. Case in point was Waste Connections (WCN), which collects, transfers, recycles, and disposes of waste for municipalities and businesses in the U.S. and Canada. Revenues exceeded expectations though earnings fell shy of them given the impacts of foreign exchange and lower prices for recycled cardboard. The resilience of Waste Connections' business within the current economic landscape contributed to a 14% increase in its stock price.

Pulling back by −19% was EMCOR Group, Inc. (EME), which provides construction and operational services for mechanical and electrical systems to a broad range of commercial, industrial, utility, and institutional customers. Although revenues were less than expected, earnings were better, and the company increased earnings guidance along with its share repurchase authorization for 2025. The underlying business trends remained strong, though there were some market concerns that EMCOR's activities tied to data center construction would slow. Previously, we had trimmed our position somewhat, seeing early signals of that sentiment shift. As EMCOR's price fell, we began adding back given the strong demand the company saw.

On a positive note, there was Verisk Analytics (vrs), the property/casualty insurance risk information and analysis provider. The company reported revenues and earnings that exceeded expectations with steady organic growth. Management was upbeat regarding strategic initiatives that should improve sales momentum and pricing, though was conservative with its initial guidance for 2025. Combined, that gave Verisk's shares an 8% lift. Among the wide variety of information technology companies, we prefer critical system providers, specialized component designers, systems that improve productivity or efficiency for their clients, and others that are growing their shares of corporate IT budgets.

Jumping ahead by 9% was JFrog Ltd. (FROG), which provides systems to manage and speed up the release of software updates from developers to users. We added to our position in February with the view that the wider creation or adoption of GenAI applications for businesses would generate greater activity volumes for JFrog. That outlook was supported by the company's subsequent report. Revenues, earnings, and forward guidance were all higher than anticipated as JFrog closed several multi-million-dollar deals along with higher rates of mid and smaller sized contracts. After that report, we met with JFrog's management and discussed its initial successes cross selling additional security services. Thus, when its price retreated in March, we bought additional shares in JFrog.

Slipping by -13% was Gartner, Inc. (IT), which offers independent and objective research and analysis on information technology, computer hardware, software, and communications. While revenues and earnings were better than expected thanks to accelerating growth in contract values, Gartner's outlook for the year was less than anticipated. From our experience, we have seen Gartner begin its fiscal years conservatively only to steadily improve its results, so we added to our position. There was weakness in Gartner's shares later in the quarter when federal government cost cutting was projected to affect about 4% of Gartner's revenues (though we believe that was a price overreaction).

We had a different view on Teradyne, Inc. (TER), a leading producer of testing and measurement equipment for semiconductors and other complex electronic systems. Although its revenues and earnings were higher than forecasted, Teradyne's management lowered its guidance for the full year. The company had presented an optimistic picture of the broad industry outlook, but was unable to translate that to its own sales expectations. On a later analyst day, Teradyne again tempered its outlook, and we exited the position as it declined by −29% for the partial quarter.

To note that the global trade outlook and its ramifications across economies are "fluid" (the operative term for many current evaluations), seems an understatement. We see companies girding for greater turbulence before the waters calm. The incorporation of a weak first quarter and the Trump administration's "shock and awe" tariff policy unveiled in early April led the U.S. Federal Reserve Board (the Fed) and the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) to reduce their U.S. and global gross domestic product (GDP) growth forecasts. All of this has created significant price swings recently. In a few instances, we may find stocks that have so dramatically oversold that they warrant us adding to those positions. At the same time, we recognize the potential for additional rapid changes in government policies, so we are cautious about overreacting. As long term investors, our approach has historically been more resilient to these types of market upheavals, and that investment style has not changed. As always, we are available for any questions you might have.

data.title: Detente By Deadline Extension
data.description:
Markets rallied as Washington and Beijing agreed not to escalate. Labor Day became the new line in the sand, and Section 899 was blindfolded and tied to a post, prompting traders to believe diplomacy might outrun duty hikes.

Level Change 6/27/25 (%)

+1.0 Dow

+0.5 Nasdaq

+0.4 Nasdaq 100

+0.5 S&P 500

+0.3 S&P 400

+0.2 S&P 600

Stocks shook off a midday stupor for a final-hour dash, closing the week on a strong note. The S&P 500 and Nasdaq set fresh records, with tariff bears and oil bulls nowhere to be found - last seen muttering about mispriced risk before disappearing into the tall grass.

Start with trade: The US and China dusted off their Geneva handshake and affirmed a framework to implement May’s grand bargain away from trade-war maximalism. Washington will loosen a few screws, while Beijing speeds up exports of the minerals that make modern life scroll.

Canada, on the other hand, got shoved back down the stairs. President Trump nixed talks over Ottawa’s digital service taxes and promised to reveal Canada’s new tariff rate within a week. Odds favor higher - and a fresh nickname. Maple Moochers, obviously.

Treasury Secretary Bessent played good cop, stretching the July 9 trade deal deadline to Labor Day. He also urged Congress to yank Section 899 from the big, beautiful bill - a Trumpian tool for retaliatory tax tantrums. “Greater certainty for the global economy,” he said, in a statement that sounded like Oppenheimer praising the disappearance of nuclear weapons.

Elsewhere in the House of Numbers and Nonsense, inflation refused to cooperate with forecasts. Core PCE rose 0.2% in May, a tick hotter than the expected 0.1%. Personal income and spending both fell. The consumer, it seems, is finally running out of rope, or at least choosing not to lasso anything new with it.

The Fed may not mind. Minneapolis’s Kashkari reiterated his call for two cuts this year, with the first possibly in September - unless tariffs hit like a hammer instead of a flyswatter. Another fine entry in the two-handed economics genre: it’ll be fine, unless it isn’t.

Which brings us to Nike (NKE +15.2%), poster child for not-great news that isn’t quite as bad as feared. Revenue fell 12%, tariffs will cost it $1B, and margins are set to shrink - but guidance for next quarter beat expectations. That was all Wall Street needed. If the swoosh can stomach the storm, maybe the rest of retail won’t drown.

And that was the day: China got coaxed, Canada got iced, consumers got cautious, and Nike got credit for running in place.

data.title: Omron Corporation: A Moaty, Diversified Industrial Firm Positioned For Secular Growth
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome AR Insights as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Introduction

The market displays excessive negativity toward Omron Corporation (OTCPK:OMRNY) because its stock performance has substantially trailed the Nikkei 225 during the past five years. Omron maintains its leadership position in industrial automation and healthcare devices and infrastructure systems despite current revenue challenges. The company benefits from persistent market trends related to labor shortages and digital healthcare and energy sustainability, which create opportunities for sustained growth. According to my assessment the company presents an opportunity to purchase shares at a discount of 31% against its actual worth.

Company Overview

As a global leader in industrial automation and healthcare technology and social infrastructure systems, Omron Corporation operates within the Electrical Equipment industry. The Japanese-based company operates from its headquarters in Kyoto and maintains 28,450 employees spread across the world as of March 2024. Through its diverse business operations Omron provides automation systems alongside health monitoring devices as well as electronic components and data-driven services. Omron Corporation operates as a Tokyo Stock Exchange (TSE: 6645) listed company and trades under the ticker symbol OMRNY in the United States through American Depositary Receipts (ADR). The company operates through five distinct business segments that make up its overall structure.

Industrial Automation (IAB) serves as the company’s largest division which generated 42.7% of net sales while producing 67.1% of operating profit. IAB delivers factory automation equipment consisting of programmable logic controllers (PLCs) sensors and motion control devices and vision systems and collaborative robots. Automotive manufacturers together with food and beverage producers and semiconductor companies utilize its products in their operations.

Social Systems, Solutions and Service (SSB) represents 18.5% of net sales while operating profit from this segment equals 31.1%. The SSB division operates infrastructure systems across Japan to deliver automated fare collection systems together with ticket vending machines and traffic control systems and solar power monitoring systems. Omron Corporation became the first company to develop an automated ticket gate system when it launched this technology in 1967.

Healthcare (HCB) generates 17.1% of net sales and delivers 32.4% of operating profit. Omron Healthcare produces digital blood pressure monitors together with thermometers and ECG devices and nebulizers and body composition analyzers. Omron Healthcare stands as the worldwide leader for digital blood pressure monitor production.

The Devices & Module Solutions (DMB) division generates 16.7% of total sales while producing 0.6% of the company's operating profit. DMB operates as an electronic component manufacturing business which produces relays and switches and connectors and MEMS sensors. These products serve multiple sectors including automotive systems together with consumer electronics and industrial equipment and mobile devices.

Data Solution Business (DSB) operates as the newest segment which generates 5.0% of net sales and 5.2% of operating profit. The DSB segment delivers data-driven services mainly through digital healthcare operations. The segment operates as a result of combining JMDC operations with medical data analysis and remote diagnostic services and insurance-related health data services.

Eliminations and Others: The segment shows intra-company adjustments together with corporate expenses which result in a 36.3% reduction of consolidated operating profit. Business units within the company eliminate internal transactions through this segment and corporate expenses remain unallocated to specific segments. Executive compensation together with centralized research and development expenses as well as information technology infrastructure and corporate office operational costs fall under this category.

The revenue breakdown of Omron shows that Japan represents its largest market with 44.3% followed by Greater China at 20.0% and Europe at 14.9% and the Americas at 10.6% and Southeast Asia and Others at 10.0%. The company gets 0.3% of its total revenue through direct exports which involve Japan-to-overseas customer sales without regional subsidiary involvement. I believe that Omron operates with sufficient geographic diversity throughout its operations.

The Tokyo-listed shares of Omron Corporation (6645.T) have performed poorly versus the Nikkei 225 during the last five years, decreasing by about 50% while the index rose by 70%. The stock has dropped to JPY 3,800 which remains close to its 52-week minimum of JPY 3,600 and significantly below its 52-week maximum of about JPY 6,800. The current stock price stands at JPY 3,800 while market sentiment appears overly negative. This combination of long-term underperformance with a low share price indicates a potential value opportunity for investors. The stock has good potential for a market revaluation when Omron's fundamentals show improvement against the broader Japanese market.

Industry Analysis

Industrial Automation & Factory Control

The global industrial automation & control systems market was valued at approximately US $206 billion in 2024 and is forecast to reach US $379 billion by 2030, growing at a CAGR of ~10.8%. The market expansion stems from increasing interest in AI predictive maintenance solutions together with IoT integration and robotic systems, especially in Asia-Pacific which made up 39% of the market during 2024.

Omron operates in the market through its IAB segment which delivers controllers, sensors, relays, vision systems, and motion control equipment. Omron occupies a position in the top PLC vendors alongside Siemens, Rockwell and ABB with approximately 8% market share.

Threat of New Entrants — Low

The establishment of industrial automation operations demands substantial financial resources for research development alongside manufacturing infrastructure and sales distribution networks. New businesses must overcome extensive product development timelines alongside certification processes and develop industrial client trust before entering this market. The industrial automation sector functions under control of established giants Siemens AG (OTCPK:SIEGY), ABB Ltd. (OTCPK:ABBNY), Rockwell Automation (ROK), Mitsubishi Corporation (OTCPK:MSBHF) together with Omron. Most new startups face a long and difficult process to establish themselves as major market players.

Supplier Power — Moderate

Specialized components' suppliers including semiconductors, sensors and precision parts have some ability to influence prices, especially during global supply chain issues. Large companies such as Omron benefit from better supplier agreements because their global operations and extensive scale enable them to secure advantageous purchasing terms.

Buyer Power – Moderate

Large industrial clients possess some negotiating power regarding prices yet Omron's equipment becomes difficult to replace due to its integrated nature and customized design and high deployment costs. The power of buyers becomes restricted after the integration process particularly in long-life automation environments.

Threat of Substitutes — Moderate

Substitutes include alternative automation approaches such as software-only solutions, cloud-based controls, or emerging AI-driven platforms reducing hardware dependency. While software and cloud solutions grow, the replacement of full hardware systems remains limited because physical sensors and PLCs and motion controllers are fundamental for operation.

Competitive Rivalry: Moderate

The market stability and product specialization alongside limited major competitors and high customer costs for switching result in reduced market competition. The rivalry operates at a moderate intensity which does not reach the intense competition found in fragmented markets such as food service or fashion retail.

Home Healthcare Devices

The global home healthcare devices market reached a value of US $178.5 billion in 2024 and is projected to grow to US $417 billion by 2032 at a Compound Annual Growth Rate (OTC:CAGR) of 11.2%. The market growth results from three key factors including an aging population and the increasing number of chronic diseases alongside the rising adoption of home monitoring solutions. North America maintains its position as the largest market but Asia-Pacific shows the fastest growth rate because healthcare services are becoming more accessible and consumers are becoming more health-conscious.

Omron dominates this sector through its Healthcare Business (HCB) segment that offers a range of FDA- and CE-cleared medical devices including blood pressure monitors, digital thermometers, electrotherapy devices (TENS units), nebulizers, body composition monitors and portable ECG devices. The company leads the market as the leading manufacturer of digital blood pressure monitors. Omron is also expanding its ecosystem with the Omron Connect app and cloud platform, integrating device data for health tracking and early intervention as part of its vision of a “Going for Zero” society—aimed at eliminating strokes and heart attacks through preventive monitoring.

Threat of New Entrants — Moderate

The entry barrier for medical-grade devices exists because manufacturers need to meet FDA and CE requirements as well as perform clinical validations. Basic health products such as thermometers and fitness trackers can be entered by new businesses at a lower regulatory threshold. The brand trust of Omron alongside its regulatory knowledge and worldwide distribution channels creates market advantages but the entry of new competitors with minimal validation requirements poses price competition threats to lower-end segments.

2. Supplier Power — Low

Omron sources commonly available components like sensors, semiconductors, and casings. The combination of Omron’s large procurement volume and its worldwide supplier network decreases the long-term influence of suppliers even though global chip shortages might create short-term difficulties.

3. Buyer Power — High

In the home healthcare device industry, buyers — primarily individual consumers — hold substantial power. Most devices are widely available, easy to replace and share standard features generating intense market competition, especially in the low- to mid-price product segment.

The market has price-sensitive customers who shift between different brands or models by checking reviews and checking prices on the internet or retail locations. The market contains many generic products along with original equipment manufacturer brands that push established companies to maintain aggressive pricing. Omron and other trusted brands benefit from consumer confidence but still are not immune to the effects of high buyer leverage in this competitive and transparent marketplace.

4. Threat of Substitutes — Moderate

Smartwatches (e.g., Apple Watch, Galaxy Watch, Fitbit) now feature ECG, blood oxygen, and heart rate variability tracking, blurring the line between consumer tech and medical monitoring. The majority of existing devices do not achieve complete medical-grade standards and fail to replace diagnostic equipment when used for regulated medical applications. That said, continued tech innovation poses an increasing threat to single-purpose medical devices, especially if future updates receive regulatory approval.

Competitive Rivalry — High

The home healthcare device market is crowded and fragmented, especially in categories like blood pressure monitors and thermometers. Numerous manufacturers—particularly from China and India—compete on price, availability, and basic functionality, intensifying rivalry in the value segment.

In the premium segment, competition is shaped by firms offering clinically validated, accurate, and reliable devices. Established players such as Omron, Microlife, and Welch Allyn (BAX) compete based on brand trust, clinical certification, and increasingly on integration with remote monitoring services. Nonetheless, Omron is likely to thrive regardless of the price competition brought on by these companies as it has garnered the trust of doctors and pharmacists — an important and sticky characteristic. It remains the #1 recommended brand for blood pressure monitors, for example.

Improving Profitability Despite Decline in Revenue

Omron’s revenue has been growing at 3.4% CAGR for the past 5 years. However, due to the recent slowdown in investment in semiconductor and EV industries and tariffs, Omron’s revenue suffered a consecutive decline of 6.8% and 2.1% in the last two fiscal years.

In response, Omron’s management has shifted their focus from top-line growth to bottom line profitability. As a result, despite a second sale decline of 2.1% in FY 2024, its operating income has rebounded by 55.9%. Based on Omron’s competitive advantages, I believe Omron would certainly outlive this recent downturn (occasional cyclical downturn is inevitable in manufacturing sectors), and strong secular trends discussed below would drive long-term growth in Omron’s earnings.

Limited Debt & Steady Dividend

Omron demonstrates strong financial discipline and a consistent commitment to return value to shareholders.

The company maintained zero debt on its balance sheet for nearly a decade until FY2023. Even after taking on debt in FY2023 and FY2024, Omron maintained a healthy balance sheet, with cash comfortably exceeding total debt in both years.

Over the past ten years, Omron has steadily increased its annual dividend from 68 JPY per share to 104 JPY per share, while maintaining a consistent level of R&D expense (ranging from 5.5% to 6.8% of sales, average at 6.3%) to maintain its competitive position in its industry areas where consistent innovation and product improvement is indispensable.

Economic Moat & Secular Tailwinds

Industrial Automation

Omron’s industrial automation segment provides a broad range of factory automation equipment including programmable logic controller (PLC), sensors, and robots. This business area’s moat stems from its switching cost and reputation.

Omron’s automation equipment is deeply integrated into manufacturers’ systems. Switching to a different supplier usually requires reprogramming software systems and retraining staff, and would increase the risk of malfunctioning. Consequently, customers tend to remain with their current supplier, contributing to sticky revenue for Omron.

Moreover, manufacturers usually require equipment with high precision and durability, so they tend to choose from providers with a long track record of supplying reliable equipment. This forms a barrier to entry into the field, enabling suppliers like Omron with decades of experience to maintain their market share.

Despite recent headwinds leading to consecutive declines in revenue for the last two fiscal years, I believe this segment would experience long-term growth due to two secular trends. First, most developed countries are experiencing the problems of an aging population and increasing cost of labour (for instance, Japan’s over‑65 demographic reached a record 29.3% in 2024), hence the need for factory automation would be even greater in the future. Second, as the Internet of Things (IOT) becomes increasingly prevalent in the manufacturing sector, Omron’s smart devices that enable collection of real-time data from machines and connection to cloud/database would experience an increase in demand.

Healthcare

The 2025 investor presentation of Omron shows that blood pressure monitors (BPMs) generate 64% of healthcare segment sales with nebulizers accounting for 15%. The company leads the global market with 45% market share of home-use BPMs and 25% market share of nebulizers.

Omron has a track record of consistent innovation in home healthcare. It introduced the first home-use blood pressure monitor in 1973 and launched the world’s first wearable BPM in 2019. In 2024, Omron received FDA De Novo clearance for its IntelliSense AFib algorithm, which is the first AI-based atrial fibrillation detection tool integrated into a home-use blood pressure monitor.

The trend toward remote diagnosis and telemedicine continues to accelerate. The global remote patient monitoring (RPM) market is projected to grow at a CAGR of 18.6%, reaching USD 16.9 billion by 2030. Omron maintains a dominant market position and continued investment in developing new products which positions it well to capture the long-term growth of connected home healthcare solutions.

Social Infrastructure Systems

The majority of revenue in this segment comes from energy solutions (e.g. storage batteries and power conditioners) (42%) and railway services (e.g. passenger gates and ticket vending machines) (24%). Omron developed the world's first automated ticket gates and ticket vending machines in 1967 and has maintained a leading market share of approximately 50% in these products in Japan.

Railway operators prioritize reliability, as equipment malfunctions during operating hours can impact passenger traffic. Consequently, they tend to use equipment vendors with a long proven track record like Omron, as switching suppliers could introduce risks.

Omron’s social infrastructure products are often integrated into broader systems of their customers, making switching suppliers costly and complex. The integration between Omron products and their customers leads to customer loyalty and customer retention.

The main driver of expansion in this segment has been energy solutions which showed a 20% CAGR during the past five years. The low penetration rate of energy storage systems and power conditioners in homes, coupled with global trends toward sustainability, should serve as secular tailwinds for Omron’s energy products.

Comparable Comps Analysis

Compared to other large-cap diversified industrial peers, Omron appears attractively priced across EV/Revenue, EV/EBITDA, and P/Tangible Book Value, trading below the peer average on each of these metrics. The only exception is its P/E ratio, which currently appears elevated at 46.4x.

The high P/E ratio does not reflect the actual core earnings power of the company. The FY2024 results were impacted by a JPY 27.8 billion one-time restructuring charge that resulted from the reduction of approximately 2,000 employees. Omron's normalized FY2024 net income would amount to JPY 44.1 billion after removing this exceptional expense which would result in a P/E ratio of 18x and align with peer companies.

DCF Model

My calculation of intrinsic equity value for Omron amounts to JPY 1,093 billion (USD 7.58 billion) but its current market capitalization stands at JPY 756 billion (USD 5.24 billion) resulting in an undervaluation of about 31%.

To account for cyclicality in Omron’s core Industrial Automation business, I adjusted operating income by applying the average operating margin over the past 10 years (8.62%) to the company’s trailing twelve-month (TTM) revenue of JPY 801.8 billion. This approach aligns valuation with Omron’s current operational scale while mitigating the impact of margin fluctuations driven by manufacturing sector downcycles.

The 3.4% growth rate to perpetuity reflects the company's five-year historical revenue CAGR of 3.4% which I believe will be a reasonable long-term estimate given , supported by structural drivers in healthcare, automation, and energy systems.

I used a cost of capital of 5.5% because Omron’s risk profile is low given its diversified revenue streams, leadership in specific markets and solid balance sheet.

The formula I used to calculate the intrinsic value is

Where growth rate divided by return on capital is used to estimate the reinvestment required to produce the growth anticipated, which is therefore subtracted from adjusted operating income to obtain free cash flow. Compare this to standard cashflow formula:

Risks

Omron’s Industrial Automation segment, which has accounted for approximately half of the company’s total revenue over the past decade, remains highly sensitive to the cyclical nature of global manufacturing activity. The demand for factory automation solutions will decrease if manufacturing sectors experience slow down and reduction in their capital spending.

Omron Healthcare continues to dominate the global market for blood pressure monitors and nebulizers but faces growing competition from other companies. The expansion of low-cost personal healthcare devices in the market makes it difficult for the company to keep prices up and differentiate its products. High-end smart health devices are increasingly dominated by technology giants like Apple (AAPL), Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF) and Xiaomi (OTCPK:XIACY) because of their strong research capabilities and complete digital systems which create a major competitive threat.

In China, Omron’s position in the industrial automation market is under pressure from both rising domestic competitors and industrial policies favoring local production. These challenges include price-based competition and government initiatives — such as “Made in China 2025” — that prioritize the development and adoption of domestically manufactured automation equipment.

End Note

I recommend a Buy on Omron Corporation, with a fair value estimate of JPY 1,093 billion (USD 7.58 billion) —representing a 31% upside from its current market capitalization. The combination of Omron's strong financial position, its disciplined approach to capital distribution and its long-term structural growth opportunities provides the basis for a valuation re-rating as the cyclical pressures subside.

data.title: Without GLP-1 Hims & Hers Is A $20s Per Share Stock -- Sell Call
data.description:
Hims & Hers (NYSE:HIMS) stock plunged almost 35% on Monday to $41.98 per share after Novo Nordisk (NVO) announced that it would be ending its collaboration with the telehealth company that put the blockbuster weight loss therapy, Wegovy, on Hims’ platform.

Hims is still up 104% YTD, priced into that is the run-up on the compounded weight loss sales allowed while original treatment was in shortage, the sell-off after original treatment was removed from the shortage list, the positives gained in late April when news of the Novo partnership broke, the loss of that partnership earlier this week, and the latest commentary from Hims' CEO Andrew Dudum reassuring investors and patients that the company would continue to "offer cheaper, compounded versions of GLP-1 weight-loss drugs." The chart below shows the trajectory of events.

Hims' stock was down roughly 50% between its February highs of $68.74 and April 29th, the day the partnership was announced, since which it surged 83% prior to Monday's crash. There is a lot of PTSD when it comes to buying the dip on Hims because of its crash in late February, which dragged the stock price down to around $23 per share. Hims is a pretty volatile stock, with more than 94 moves over 5% over the past year, and even investors' positive reaction to Dudum's reassurance this week, with the stock trading over 19% on Thursday, confirms it. We think investors should stay away for now because while the negative of losing the deal is priced into the stock, it's not priced into 1. the outlook, or 2. the average revenue per subscriber.

We also have little visibility on how Hims will continue to meet patient requirements for weight-loss drugs without the Novo partnership. Even if we do assume the worst is priced in, we don't see any near-term tailwinds to drive upside into the next quarter. We recommend investors wait until the stock visits the EMA200 level of $36 per share before thinking about jumping back in.

Part of why Monday’s sell-off was so intense is because the breakup wasn’t just a deal termination; it also was full of drama, which we cannot get into here due to the accusatory nature of the claims thrown around. However, we think it's safe to say that Novo and Hims are unlikely to team up anytime soon. In fact, Novo has already courted another company, WeightWatchers, to spur access to Wegovy. This not only confirms that Novo is already moving on, but it also makes the market a lot more competitive for Hims, considering that starting July 1st, WeightWatchers customers can access Wegovy through CenterWell pharmacy with introductory pricing of $299 and $199 for those getting a refill.

The Novo partnership was crucial for Hims to continue its growth trajectory, with its outperformance largely reliant on its ability to tap into the legal and affordable weight-loss treatment. Keep in mind that on the Q1 earnings call, Dudum stressed:

Teaming up with Novo Nordisk is a pivotal milestone.

Now that the partnership is off the table, all the positives it was supposed to bring to Hims' financials are too.

Timing of earnings matters, a lot

If we look at Hims’ financials, the company last reported in May for the quarter ended March 31st, and guided FY25 sales in the range of $2.3B to $2.4B versus consensus for $2.32B and for Q2 sales of $530M-$550M versus estimates for $564.57M. The stock took a hit on the report, although subscribers grew 38% to 2.4M and sales were up 111% to $586M. The quarter didn’t price in the Novo deal positives, but the outlook, reported after the deal announcement, could very well be priced in. The deal was a big topic of conversation on the call. The chart below outlines the company’s trajectory of sales, operating margin, and subscribers.

When it comes to the near-term, we do think that there is a higher risk heading into the next print because 1.) Outlook could have priced in some of the Novo attraction to customers, and more importantly, 2.) We don’t know what Hims’ financials will look like without its stronghold on GLP-1.

Remember, the stock surge in February was because of its benefit from the weight loss compound being on the shortage list, and that’s why it drove the stock. Then, even in Q1, the numbers reported reflected the benefit of the ramped sale of cheaper copies, since the quarter ended in March, and the FDA prohibited the sales of copies as of May 22nd.

The metrics we care about at the end of the day are two: subscriber growth and monthly online revenue per average subscriber. While subscriber growth is steadily on the rise with data indicating increased traffic on the site, average revenue per user wasn’t until all the weight-loss drama entered the conversation. In Q1, that number hit $84, up 52.7% Y/Y from $73 the quarter prior in Q4, and $67 in Q3, and prior to that was constrained within the $50s range without a material uptick, as shown below.

This makes us more concerned about the trajectory of average revenue per subscriber, considering it was only carried higher on the weight loss drugs, specifically the cheaper copies.

What to do with the stock?

Hims has taken a nosedive that has de-risked it from losing the partnership with Novo; however, the risk now is on worsening financials without having a solid foot in the weight loss treatment market. We see some more downside in the near term before it's time to revisit the stock when it’s below $40 per share, edging closer to its EMA200 support of $36.20.

Hims isn't going anywhere, and we do think this player will make a comeback in the mid to long term due to the fact that Hims is much bigger than GLP-1 (Glucagon-like peptide-1), which is the compounded semaglutide used in weight loss medications, Ozempic and Wegovy. The company is exploring new ventures into menopause and testosterone, which are piled on top of Hims’ treatments in: weight loss, mental health, hers derm, hims derm, and sexual health. These two could be massive markets to tap into, with the menopause treatment market estimated to grow at a CAGR of 4.8% and testosterone at 4%, respectively. This makes us more positive on the Hims in the mid-to-long term, but we do think the market needs to reset expectations for the stock and its access to weight-loss drug tailwinds in the near term.
